```sql
CREATE VIEW SalesSummary AS
SELECT p.name as product_name, SUM(s.quantity) as total_quantity, SUM(s.amount) as total_revenue
FROM Sale s
JOIN Product p ON s.product_id = p.product_id
GROUP BY p.name;
```

Instead of building the query step-by-step with method chaining, I can pass the entire query structure directly to the database query method. This means constructing the select clause with explicit column references and table aliases, then joining the tables and mapping the results to TimeMetrics.

```cpp
std::vector<TimeMetrics> project_time_tracking() {
  sqlgen::sqlite::Database db("test_db.sqlite");
  
  return db.query<TimeMetrics>(
    sqlgen::select(
      sqlgen::column<Project>("project_id", "p"),
      sqlgen::column<Project>("name", "p"),
      sqlgen::day(sqlgen::column<TimeLog>("start_time", "t")),
      sqlgen::month(sqlgen::column<TimeLog>("start_time", "t")),
      sqlgen::year(sqlgen::column<TimeLog>("start_time", "t")),
      sqlgen::hour(sqlgen::column<TimeLog>("start_time", "t")),
      sqlgen::minute(sqlgen::column<TimeLog>("start_time", "t")),
      sqlgen::second(sqlgen::column<TimeLog>("start_time", "t")),
      sqlgen::days_between(
        sqlgen::column<TimeLog>("start_time", "t"),
        sqlgen::column<TimeLog>("end_time", "t")
      )
    )
    .from<Project>("p")
    .inner_join<TimeLog>("t", "p.project_id = t.project_id")
  );
}
```

The function opens a SQLite database connection and constructs a query that joins the Project and TimeLog tables, extracting temporal components from the start_time field along with the duration between start and end times. The results are mapped directly to TimeMetrics objects.
